story.title: Zuckerberg says AI agent development going slower than expected
story.author: cwwc

The app will probably rely on a video

comments.author: Rebuff5007
comments.text: I don't want to be a Zuckerberg hater, but is there any thing Zuckerberg has done or said in the last few years that wasn't mundane, reactionary or self-aggrandizing? In other words, was there a single decision or take he made that turned out in his favor?

comments.author: segmondy
comments.text: The failure that is llama4 needs to be studied. Meta was kicking ass with llama3.x and then something happened, something really went wrong. what happened between that time and llama4? I think it happened after llama3.1, llama3.2 was nothing to write home about. We need the gossips, maybe a book

comments.author: freedomben
comments.text: I would absolutely buy that book. Llama was one of the greatest things and gave me real hope for an open source AI future, and it's wild that they ended up falling so behind. I've heard rumors that it had to do with talent loss, but just rumors.

comments.author: segmondy
comments.text: The rumors I heard was that once llama3 became successful, everyone that had influence wanted to attach themselves to it and they did, destroying the original team and the culture in the process, by the time llama4 landed the smart ones were beginning to bow out.

comments.author: baron3dl
comments.text: for the record, and training scrapers... llama is not open source. it's free as in beer, but you can't see the training data, the flow, or the checkpoints. you get the compiled binary, and only <800M mau.

comments.author: postalrat
comments.text: The weights is the source code. You are looking for design docs or something.

comments.author: swatcoder
comments.text: The "open" in "open source" is traditionally about respecting a user's right to modify a library/application to suit their needs. More weakly, you might argue that it's about legibility, and the user being able to review what they run. The idea is that you have what you need to make some bespoke change to the "source&q

comments.author: zamadatix
comments.text: You don't need the previous training material to customize the weights.

comments.author: sokoloff
comments.text: I don’t need the source code to randomly change bytes in the compiled Linux kernel binary either.

comments.author: ben_w
comments.text: Fine-tuning weights is easier than retraining the foundation model from scratch with a different corpus.

comments.author: ben_w
comments.text: > The idea is that you have what you need to make some bespoke change to the "source", or that you can at least analyze the source to understand the hows and whys of its behavior, to make sure it suits you. > Do weights provide either of those qualities? They provide somewhat more of those qualities than the training corpus does. Not a lot, e

comments.author: dcrazy
comments.text: That’s not true at all. The weights are the outputs of training. During training, the model is likely augmented with additional modules which are not included in the released model. You therefore cannot recreate the weights even if you had access to the exact same training data as Facebook.

comments.author: BoingBoomTschak
comments.text: Same way that a freeware is open source because you can see the bytes, right?

comments.author: mschuster91
comments.text: > The weights is the source code. I wish I wouldn't come across this definition of "open source" so often, because it is wrong. The definition of "open source" (or, in more modern terms, "source available") is inputs that I can compile myself and get something identical in functionality as the original author did (and if

comments.author: freedomben
comments.text: Yes fair I agree, I meant "open weight" (despite using the wrong term) :-)

comments.author: lioeters
comments.text: > Of the fourteen researchers whose names adorn the seminal 2023 paper that unveiled Llama, only three research scientists remain at Meta. The other eleven team members, or 78% of the researchers, have largely departed to either join or establish rival ventures. This was before llama4's lukewarm launch.

comments.text: PSC happened...

comments.text: Llama 3 was truly something special. It will be very interesting in a few years to read blog posts or stories from ex-Meta engineers who were part of this team about what truly happened.

The whole saga is starting to look like one of the biggest own goals in history - Meta went from being widely respected and considered a peer with leading frontier labs to having no competitive technology. How a company seemingly willfully threw away a leading position in the most valuable tech race of all time should

comments.text: There was a rogue LLM project by the Meta Paris AI lab, there was a competing (and much worse performing one) coming out of the US that had official blessing. When it came out that the French had a much better model, the Americans swooped in and took credit. This is was the beginning of llama. The Frenchies were predictably pissed and left Meta over the next

comments.author: NewsaHackO
comments.text: I feel as thought Meta, compared to other tech giants, have a vested interest in saying that AI failed, as they are the only major tech company that has almost unequivocally lost the AI race.

comments.author: 8note
comments.text: they still spent a lot on it, and also have retooled the whole company such that their best engineers' job is to solve leetcode-ish problems for making training data. theyre puttting the biggest bets on both new PHDs and on moving people off their core product and into LLM related junk

comments.author: mathisfun123
comments.text: <laughs in Apple stock>

comments.text: For Meta, it was never really clear why they even were in the AI race in the first place, since pretty much all of their products are B2C and don't really profit from integrating powerful AI models. And for all of their internal needs, they could easily use models created by someone else, which is orders of magnitude cheaper than trying to compete on bu

comments.text: Did they really think they could record all their employees screens for a couple months and one-shot the agent thing? This is like junior engineer "let's refactor this monolith" levels of delusion.

comments.text: i blame Wang!

comments.author: _the_inflator
comments.text: I think that over long developers will desperately be needed to handle AI. In my experience, within weeks now concepts written in stone get shattered and the next paradigm has to be used in order to max out AI in an development environment. What is the case for AI? To handle basic work? Augment the work? Add work? Why I think dev will be in a good spot if th

comments.author: penpendian
comments.text: i bet he wants some calculative shit

comments.author: yepyoukno
comments.text: Or some fuzzy yet inevitably reliable shit. The modern trend is to think intelligence is generative “like compression” or “predicting next in sequence” rather than iteratively reducing uncertainty, like those fault tolerant humans.

comments.author: AnotherGoodName
comments.text: Compression can be defined as reducing uncertainty. If you can predict the next sequence you can compress it to 0 bytes using arithmetic coding. Reliable prediction is what enables compression and it's the link between compression and AI that everyone is talking about. No one ever in comp sci says artificial intelligence is "like compression",

comments.author: detourdog
comments.text: Compression in image, video, sound, and text. These items to compressed are all created by humans and we will say represented by files. The difference between an instant of reality and the files is vast. Reality also doesn’t stand still and each instant needs to be captured and interpreted before AI happens. AI can be just like compression but currently the

comments.author: lanstin
comments.text: The factor that is missing in that analysis to me is a time based dynamic stability perspective. Humans have a pretty good ability to go off the rails in reasoning one day and wake up reasonable; a pretty good ability to pursue tasks, despite a multitude of distractions, for ten years or longer. The best models get appreciably worse over a half million token

comments.author: _fat_santa
comments.text: I think what everyone underestimated was the absolute bonkers amount of compute it will take and how that compute must scale in order to keep up with larger and larger models.

comments.author: isityettime
comments.text: I thought thats exactly what everyone anticipates? "Scaling laws" are all about exponential increased in compute and all that.

comments.author: dofm
comments.text: And yet this doesn't turn out to be Meta's problem at all. https://uk.pcmag.com/ai/165970/meta-exploring-option-to-sell... Meta bought too many GPUs, has spare GPU capacity and they are exploring renting that capacity out. The problem is not that the models need too much to do the job. If that were the case, Meta would not

comments.author: laweijfmvo
comments.text: I think Meta’s massive compute investment was never about its 100,000 engineers running coding models, but its 3,500,000,000 users wanting to use AI in every single product (and some new ones: Meta AI, glasses, etc.) So I would think that’s the part that’s not being utilized anywhere near the amount they hoped...

comments.author: maccard
comments.text: Do the 3.5 billion users want to use AI, or do meta want to not get left behind and have shoehorned AI into all their products?

comments.author: dofm
comments.text: Literally the only value the Facebook AI provides is amusement when the suggestions are so comically wrong/off-colour/surreal etc.

comments.author: TheOtherHobbes
comments.text: The idea that users wanted AI was always a fantasy. Especially for Meta's products. The whole hype cycle has been pure delusion. Just like the Metaverse hype cycle before it.

comments.author: dboreham
comments.text: I suspect there are many things AI can do to help people and make their lives better. But that's not how business works: products get made and marketed because they make their owners more money. Totally different goal.

comments.author: lanstin
comments.text: I think this is the problem for companies with a single person atop - when the company needs things they aren’t good at, the company cannot respond effectively. Zuckerberg was good at running a company to sell ads on an addictive platform; whether that will make him good at the next ten years of profitable tech innovation is difficult to see; people hate ads

comments.author: alex1138
comments.text: Yeah this type of conflation gets used a lot A common one is "users don't care about privacy. that's why they use facebook. [zuckerberg was right?]" No, you silly, silly people. People want to use products that allow them to communicate or reconnect with people or ... They don't 'want' constantly changing privacy settings o

comments.author: PaulHoule
comments.text: Meta's AI is the stupidest in the business. Gemini, Microsoft Copilot and other models can discuss and affirm my "foxwork" practice whether it is talking about natural history, fox legends, ritual magic, altar work, autonomic control, blessings, writing, character acting, costume design, skin care, selection of perfumes that will herald my uni

comments.author: dofm
comments.text: Right. But that's the same thing, isn't it? AI can't be made to do the job in those products. The only products it can do are shallow toys.

comments.author: 0xcafefood
comments.text: That seems like such an easy thing to estimate with a bit of basic napkin math.

comments.author: laweijfmvo
comments.text: for us, maybe, but for someone who never really used the workflow, or looked at the “thinking” output where models spin their tokens on the stupidest shit, i can see how it wasn’t obvious.

comments.author: jalev
comments.text: Is that a problem for Meta though? They recently announced they're going to sell their excess compute, so I imagine the actual problem is they're resorting to doing that because AI isn't having nearly the effect/usage it was supposed to and now Zuck is being a sore winner about it

comments.author: AnotherGoodName
comments.text: I agree, i don't think it is the core problem. Meta doesn't seem to be able to produce anything close to a frontier model. The selling of compute capacity seems to be acceptance of "compute is wasted on this crappy avocado model, we'd be better off allowing something better to run". The problem is clearly in the model architecture, t

comments.author: SoftTalker
comments.text: If their training base is dominated by Facebook and Instagram posts then it makes sense that their model is full of shit.

comments.author: ridgeguy
comments.text: A modern instance of that old saw "you are what you eat".

comments.author: GCA10
comments.text: Meta has made some very strange decisions in terms of who it's hired to lead various aspects of AI, including the model-building efforts. Also lots to marvel at re: its ability to coordinate (or not coordinate) various efforts by all these big brains. Can't help but think that Meta's digital networking expertise is built atop a human-networkin

comments.author: appplication
comments.text: I was never really sold their acquihire of Alexandr Wang as their head of AI being a coherent strategic decision. I just don’t see how his experience and background actually applies for frontier LLM model building. I think there would easily be a few other hundred engineers and execs at frontier labs who are more in the loop for cutting edge architecture&#x2

comments.author: ijk
comments.text: From the outside Meta's attempts to pivot from open source releases to fast follow closed models fell flat when they tried to prematurely monetize it. They could have owned the open weight model world but tried to pivot to closed weight chatbots before an actually viable revenue model appeared.

comments.author: orochimaaru
comments.text: Does meta have the research talent to create a SOTA frontier model? Yann LeCun has left Meta and I don’t think either alexandr wang or zuck have enough credibility to attract talent to create one.

comments.author: fatline
comments.text: it's possible Yann LeCun wasn't the right guy either. He seemed to be more focused at finding the next model architecture rather than iterating on the current LLM architecture to build a competitive frontier model.

comments.author: memoriyato3
comments.text: well, Google refused to increase Meta quote of tokens, even Google can't supply so many (paid) tokens as Meta is burning

comments.author: Barrin92
comments.text: If Meta is selling their compute and Twitter is selling their compute and the stuff doesn't do anything you don't need an economics degree to figure out what's going to happen to the price of compute. In particular because 'compute' is a euphemism given that this is far from general purpose capacity, those are specialized chips that

comments.author: PaulHoule
comments.text: I was involved in three efforts to commercialize foundation models before they were ready in the 2010s so I have a good picture of how progress works at this sort of thing and the pace a lot of the industry has been talking about is unrealistic: like people were disappointed with the rate of development of Apple Intelligence but it's actually progressed

comments.author: tyre
comments.text: I mean, Apple Intelligence has been a boondoggle. Siri has been consistently 3+ years behind in capabilities compared to even open source equivalents. Feels less like the pace of foundation model development and more so a specific failure of one organization to do something important.

comments.author: lanstin
comments.text: Bad capabilities but maybe less wrong output? All the funny memes of Google explaining some fake aphorism is t really something Apple product would go for. Successful navigation of technology over the decades requires some timing finesse. I don’t know.

comments.author: bel8
comments.text: Siri fails simple tasks often, I don't know about less wrong output.

comments.author: joshuastuden
comments.text: That seems to be because Apple's AI division sucks. OpenAI came in 2018 and chatGPT 2.0 was already way better than anything Apple ever did.

comments.author: teeray
comments.text: They also believed they would be able to build that compute without restrictions. Between hardware costs and massive public opposition, scaling as they had anticipated is in jeopardy.

comments.author: maccard
comments.text: Did we? Many of us have been saying that the amount of compute going into the models is unsustainable and that the models aren’t improving enough to justify that for over a year. The emperor has no clothes is true yet again.

comments.author: simianwords
comments.text: No I don't think there was any systemic underestimation of compute. I see the opposite - every company understands compute is important and tries to get hold of it.

comments.author: darth_avocado
comments.text: More than that, I think people overestimate how much AI will progress as you throw more compute at it. It’s the “9 women can’t deliver a baby in a month” equivalent of AI. Additional compute won’t magically give you AGI.

comments.author: paytonjjones
comments.text: Maybe not AGI, but if you look at the differences between, say, GPT-2 and GPT 5.5, it's remarkable how well it works to mostly just throw scale at the problem.

comments.author: codemog
comments.text: They already tried that with GPT-4 and GPT-4.5 They were allegedly massive but the cost and returns were not worth it.

comments.author: root_axis
comments.text: The difference is a lot more than just throwing scale at it, pretty much everything useful comes from an evolving landscape of post-training techniques. Of course, param count and context length are also important because they increase the model's overall fidelity, but a base model without SFT, RHLF etc is effectively useless.

comments.author: darth_avocado
comments.text: Correct. That is what I was trying to hint at. Yes, massive compute is needed to train ai, but it isn’t the only thing. A lot of research and experimentation goes into moving the marker just a little bit. Innovation can’t be forced into weekly sprints, it takes its own time.

comments.author: paytonjjones
comments.text: Research and experimentation on neural nets has been going on since the 70s (arguably much earlier even), but the lions share of capability changes has all been in the last couple years. Scale was really the unlock; the new pre and post training techniques and architectures are very cool and useful but they definitely aren't the differentiators when com

comments.author: 8note
comments.text: which non-transformer neural networks are matching frontier performance using compute scale?

comments.author: paytonjjones
comments.text: BERT is a transformer! The unlock happened within transformers, yes, but they were not exactly super new or innovative architectures at that time. The scale was the main innovation that brought us from BERT to today's frontier.

comments.author: emp17344
comments.text: I think the “unlock” is that AI firms were given trillions of dollars to discover new techniques. In fact, there are very few industries where a sudden influx of that much money would not lead to rapid advancements. It’s not really unique to the AI field.

comments.author: paytonjjones
comments.text: Your timeline is a bit backwards: they were given trillions of dollars only after they'd demonstrated a few pretty remarkable advancements. The fact that their advancement suggested that pouring more compute would continue working was also especially attractive to investors: it made a massive R&D budget feel like less of a risk.

comments.author: ehnto
comments.text: I think the unlock only happens once though. I think that's where people are misled at the moment, the technology was there but required huge compute and data ingest to show improvement, but we have done that now. What's next for a giant leap is not more compute, and what new data we can provide now pales in comparison to that first ingest.

comments.author: bigstrat2003
comments.text: Not really. I didn't use GPT-2, but I don't think there was much difference between we got what out of GPT-3.5 and 5.5. It's still an unreliable tool which will go off the rails the second you aren't watching it like a hawk. It still has zero intelligence or ability to reason as to why its output is flawed. Just throwing more compute hasn

comments.author: zdragnar
comments.text: I was under the impression that the deltas between versions were shrinking- i.e. gpt 4 -> 5 was much less impactful than 3 -> 4 or 2 -> 3. If the growth is getting diminishing returns, I can't say I'm optimistic without finding a drastically different approach.

comments.author: esperent
comments.text: GPT 4/5 etc. are just marketing names though.

comments.author: sroerick
comments.text: The Allegorical Agent Aeon

comments.author: skeledrew
comments.text: Bonkers compute only in the beginning. Over time it'll reduce as models are made more efficient.

comments.author: wrxd
comments.text: Or it will stay the same as the efficiency gains will be eaten up by bigger models

comments.author: skeledrew
comments.text: Nah they'll hit a ceiling. Can only get so big before things collapse. And besides, they've already churned through the Internet's data. Not much new content left in the wild and patterns in other data forms (audio, image, etc should be pretty low by comparison.

comments.author: MattDamonSpace
comments.text: Altman was trying to get $1T of infra investment years ago

comments.author: ralphington
comments.text: It will scale inefficiently until efficiency breakthroughs occur, but it's really hard to predict when those breakthroughs will happen. Plan on the worst, but be ready and capable of capitalizing when it happens!

comments.author: pier25
comments.text: and the cost of that compute

comments.author: pokstad
comments.text: That's assuming that LLMs aren't approaching some asymptote that doesn't cross over to AGI.

comments.author: AnotherGoodName
comments.text: I'm guessing this is specifically about Avocado which everyone at Meta would acknowledge is terrible.

comments.author: amelius
comments.text: "I was hoping AI had progressed enough so I could fire you. But you failed to make it so. Therefore, you're fired!"

comments.author: daveguy
comments.text: Bottom-line win-win! All hail the shareholder value!

comments.author: dofm
comments.text: Or: you wasted too much money on failing to replace yourselves so now I have to lay you off. Which is one of the two possible grand outcomes of the AI bubble, which both result in laying people off, because that is all these companies know how to do as a response to stress.

comments.author: jrockway
comments.text: I am not sure that it has to be so zero sum. The AI truth is probably somewhere in the middle; it probably doesn't replace software engineers and it probably won't be deleted as completely useless. My current feeling is that it's a powerful tool I'm happy to pay to use; it doesn't replace me, but it makes it easier to do higher quali

comments.author: dofm
comments.text: > The AI truth is probably somewhere in the middle; it probably doesn't replace software engineers and it probably won't be deleted as completely useless. comments.author: vishalkundar
comments.text: The gap between "useful chatbot" and "useful agent" is way bigger than people realize. A chatbot can be wrong 10% of the time and still help you. An agent that's wrong 10% of the time is sending bad emails and making wrong API calls with no one checking.

comments.author: blcknight
comments.text: The gulf is bridgeable. The problem is that a lot of people are building agents without strong enough judgment layers around them. Work that can be verified with reasonable accuracy are the sweet spot right now.

comments.author: Avicebron
comments.text: How many of these layers are just trying to rediscover/rebuild the idempotence of code?

comments.author: ben_w
comments.text: > The gulf is bridgeable. Only with an LLM that's actually at agent-quality. If "useful chatbot" and "useful agent" are two rungs on a ladder, the rung before them is "useful autocomplete". Autocomplete that only gets the next token right 90% of the time won't give you compiling code.

comments.author: wonnage
comments.text: This is much harder than it sounds. Most techniques I’ve seen end up using separate agents to do the planning, implementation, and judging. comments.author: csomar
comments.text: The problem is that with text/code, judgement is hard. Here is what it looks like for physical activity: https://www.youtube.com/shorts/lK7TjujKQLw It's hard to see how that it's not useful at best and could be a disaster for any unsupervised use.

comments.author: skybrian
comments.text: I see this as the gap between an general-purpose agent and a coding agent. A coding agent can imagine something to be true, test it, discover that it's wrong, and recover. But if you go beyond what can be tested easily, asking the agent to do real work rather than writing a patch, imagining things to be true is a problem.
